section.title: Robbing God in Tithes
section.sectionRationale: The fifth disputation rests on God's unchanging faithfulness: because I the LORD do not change, you are not destroyed. On that ground God calls the people to return, and when they ask how, He names their withholding of tithes and offerings as robbery of God, inviting them to test Him with wholehearted giving and see His blessing poured out.
section.thesis: Because God does not change, He keeps calling His faithless people to return; their withheld tithes reveal hearts that rob God, but He invites them to test His faithfulness with obedient giving and promises blessing beyond measure.
section.frameNote: Two beats: the unchanging God who calls His people to return, and the charge of robbing God in tithes and offerings, with the striking invitation to test God and watch the windows of heaven open.

units.label: I the LORD do not change
units.note: return to Me, and I will return to you

verses.text: “Because I, the LORD, do not {{shanah|change}}, you descendants of Jacob have not been destroyed.

Yet from the days of your fathers, you have turned away from My statutes and have not kept them. {{shuv|Return}} to Me, and I will return to you,” says the LORD of Hosts. “But you ask, ‘How can we return?’

units.label: Will a man rob God
units.note: test Me, and see the windows of heaven open

verses.text: Will a man {{qaba|rob}} God? Yet you are robbing Me! But you ask, ‘How do we rob You?’ In {{maaser|tithes}} and offerings.
You are cursed with a curse, yet you, the whole nation, are still robbing Me.

Bring the full tithe into the storehouse, so that there may be food in My house. {{bachan|Test}} Me in this,” says the LORD of Hosts. “See if I will not open the windows of heaven and pour out for you blessing without measure.
I will rebuke the devourer for you, so that it will not destroy the fruits of your land, and the vine in your field will not fail to produce fruit,” says the LORD of Hosts.
“Then all the nations will call you blessed, for you will be a land of delight,” says the LORD of Hosts.

shanah.gloss: To change, to alter, to become different. God declares I do not change, pointing to His constancy in character and covenant. This unchanging faithfulness is the reason a faithless people has not been consumed, and it is the ground on which He keeps calling them to return.

shuv.gloss: To turn, return, repent. The great prophetic word for repentance: return to Me, and I will return to you. It describes a turning of the whole life back to God, and the mutual movement pictures a God who draws near to those who draw near to Him.

qaba.gloss: To rob, defraud, take by force what belongs to another. God charges that in withholding tithes the people were actually robbing Him. The word is startling: it reframes stinginess toward God not as a minor lapse but as theft from the One who owns all.

maaser.gloss: A tenth, a tithe. Under the law the tithe supported the priests, the temple, and the poor. Here the people's failure to bring the full tithe left God's house without provision and exposed hearts that trusted their possessions more than the God who gave them.

bachan.gloss: To test, prove, examine, as metal is assayed. God rarely invites testing, yet here He does: test Me in this. It is not a licence for greedy bargaining but an invitation to prove His faithfulness by wholehearted obedience and watch Him provide.

basic.context: God grounds His appeal in His own unchanging character: because I do not change, you have not been destroyed. He calls the people to return, and when they ask how, He points to their tithes. They had been withholding what belonged to God, and He calls it robbery. Then He gives a remarkable challenge: bring the full tithe and test Me, and see whether I do not pour out blessing.
basic.hermeneutics: This passage is often misused as a formula for guaranteed wealth. Read rightly, it addresses a specific covenant people under the law of the tithe, and its heart is trust: will they trust God enough to give obediently? The New Testament calls for generous, cheerful, sacrificial giving rather than a fixed percentage, but the principle stands that we cannot out-give God.
basic.originalLanguages: God declares He does not shanah, change, which is why He keeps calling His people to shuv, return. They had been withholding the maaser, the tithe, which God calls qaba, robbing Him. Then He invites them to bachan, to test Him, by bringing the full tithe and watching heaven open.
basic.typology: God's unchanging faithfulness that spares an undeserving people points to the steadfast grace fulfilled in Christ, the same yesterday, today, and forever. The storehouse filled so there is food in God's house anticipates the abundance of the gospel, where God supplies every need in Christ.
basic.christ: The unchanging God of verse 6 is revealed in Jesus Christ, who is the same yesterday, today, and forever, Hebrews 13:8. The one who would not let His people be destroyed is the God who so loved the world that He gave His Son, the ultimate proof that we cannot out-give God. Christ, who was rich yet became poor for us, teaches us to give freely as we have freely received.
basic.god: God is unchanging, and that constancy is pure mercy: it is why sinners are not consumed. He is faithful, calling His wayward people home again and again. He is generous, ready to pour out blessing without measure. And He is worthy of our trust, inviting His people to prove His faithfulness by obedient giving.
basic.crossReferences: Hebrews 13:8 says Jesus Christ is the same yesterday, today, and forever, the unchanging God of verse 6. Second Corinthians 9 teaches cheerful, generous giving and God's abundant supply. Luke 6:38 promises that the measure we give will be given back, pressed down and overflowing.
basic.oneStory: God's unchanging faithfulness is the thread that holds the whole Bible together and keeps a rebellious people from destruction. That constancy runs straight to Christ, the same forever, in whom God pours out blessing without measure on all who return to Him.

context.literary: The fifth disputation, opening with the great declaration I do not change and the call to return, then moving to the charge of robbing God and the invitation to test Him. It stands between the disputation on God's justice and the disputation on the faithful remnant, showing how a returning people express their repentance in concrete obedience.
context.historical: In the struggling post-exilic economy, people were tempted to keep back the tithes that supported the temple, priests, and poor. Nehemiah 13 records the same neglect, with the Levites forced to abandon temple service to farm because the tithes were not brought. Malachi confronts this withholding as theft from God and a symptom of a deeper failure to return to Him.
context.canonical: The tithe laws stem from Leviticus 27 and Numbers 18, and the storehouse recalls the temple treasuries of Nehemiah 13. The principle of testing God's provision is balanced in the New Testament by teaching on cheerful, proportionate generosity in 2 Corinthians 8-9, and God's unchanging nature is affirmed in Hebrews 13:8 and James 1:17.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To ground God's appeal in His unchanging faithfulness, to call the people to return to Him, to expose their withheld tithes as robbery of God, and to invite them to trust and obey by bringing the full tithe, with the promise that God would pour out abundant blessing.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The specific command to bring the full tithe to the storehouse was given to Israel under the Mosaic covenant. The abiding principles are that God owns all we have, that withholding from Him betrays a lack of trust, and that He is a generous provider who cannot be out-given. This is not a promise that every gift yields financial return.

debates.question: How should test Me in verse 10 be applied today?

views.label: A prosperity formula
views.case: Some read it as a guarantee that giving money to God produces financial return, so believers give in order to get; this treats the verse as a transaction.

views.label: An invitation to trust the faithful Provider
views.case: The verse invites a specific covenant people to prove God's faithfulness by obedient tithing, revealing hearts that trust Him; the New Testament reframes giving as cheerful and generous, not a lever for wealth.

debates.bearing: The passage calls us to trust God's provision and give freely, not to bargain for riches; the God who cannot be out-given still delights when His people trust Him with their resources.

study.originalLanguages: The disputation is anchored in one great truth: God does not shanah, change. His unchanging character is why the descendants of Jacob have not been consumed, and it is the basis for His call to shuv, to return, with the tender promise, return to Me and I will return to you. When they ask how, God names the maaser, the tithe, they have withheld, and stuns them by calling it qaba, robbing God. Then He does what He almost never does: He invites them to bachan, to test Him, by bringing the full tithe into the storehouse, promising to open the windows of heaven and pour out blessing without measure. The heart of it all is trust in an unchanging, generous God.

typology.type: The unchanging God who spares an undeserving people
typology.fulfillment: Jesus Christ, the same yesterday, today, and forever, in whom God's steadfast mercy is revealed, Hebrews 13:8 and Malachi 3:6.

typology.type: The storehouse with food in God's house
typology.fulfillment: The abundant provision of the gospel, where God supplies every need according to His riches in Christ, Philippians 4:19.
The overflowing grace given in Christ, in whom God freely gives us all things, Romans 8:32 and 2 Corinthians 9:8.

study.christ: The unchanging LORD of verse 6, who would not let His faithless people be destroyed, is the God revealed in Jesus Christ, who is the same yesterday, today, and forever. His constancy is our only hope: it is why sinners are spared and called home again and again. The God who invites His people to test His generosity is the God who did not spare His own Son but gave Him up for us all, the supreme proof that we can never out-give Him. Christ, who was rich yet for our sake became poor so that we through His poverty might become rich, teaches His people to give freely because they have freely received, trusting the Father who pours out blessing without measure.
study.god: God is unchanging in character and covenant, and that constancy is sheer mercy, the reason a rebellious people has not been consumed. He is patient and welcoming, forever calling His people to return with the promise that He will return to them. He is generous beyond measure, ready to open the windows of heaven, rebuke the devourer, and make His people a land of delight. And He is trustworthy, inviting them to prove His faithfulness by wholehearted obedience.

crossReferences.note: Jesus Christ is the same yesterday, today, and forever, revealing the unchanging God of verse 6.
The same neglect of tithes that left the Levites without support, the historical backdrop to Malachi's charge.
God loves a cheerful giver and is able to make all grace abound, reframing giving as generous trust rather than a formula.
The God who spared His people did not spare His own Son, the ultimate proof that we cannot out-give Him.

study.oneStory: God's unchanging faithfulness is the ground beneath the whole story of Scripture, the reason a wayward people is spared and continually called home. Malachi shows that returning to this God takes concrete shape in trusting Him with our resources rather than robbing Him. That constancy and generosity are fulfilled in Christ, the same forever, through whom God pours out blessing without measure on all who return to Him.

sermon.title: Malachi's Message On Stewardship (Malachi's Modern Message Part 6)
sermon.why: The sixth message of Legge's expository series through Malachi, covering 3:7-12, the heart of this disputation. It handles the call to return, the charge of robbing God, and the invitation to test Him, guarding against a prosperity reading and pointing to the generous, unchanging God revealed in Christ.

quiz.q: On what truth does God ground His appeal in this passage?

options.text: That He does not change, which is why His faithless people have not been destroyed.
options.note: Verse 6 makes God's unchanging character the basis of His mercy and His call.

options.text: That the people had earned a second chance.
options.note: Their survival rests on God's constancy, not their merit.

options.text: That the temple was now complete.
options.note: The ground is God's unchanging nature, not the finished building.

options.text: That the harvest had already improved.
options.note: The blessing is promised as they obey; the ground is God's changelessness.

quiz.q: How were the people robbing God?

options.text: By withholding the tithes and offerings that belonged to Him.
options.note: Verse 8 names tithes and offerings as the theft from God.

options.text: By stealing from the temple treasury directly.
options.note: The robbery was withholding what was owed, not breaking into the treasury.

options.text: By refusing to offer any sacrifices at all.
options.note: The charge is specifically about withheld tithes and offerings.

options.text: By overpaying the priests.
options.note: The opposite: they were keeping back what God required.

quiz.q: What is striking about God's words test Me in this?

options.text: God, who rarely invites testing, calls His people to prove His faithfulness by obedient giving.
options.note: Verse 10 offers a rare invitation to test God's generosity through obedience.

options.text: God is offering a formula guaranteeing personal wealth.
options.note: It calls for trust and obedience, not a transaction for riches.

options.text: God is daring them to sin and see what happens.
options.note: He invites obedience, not sin; the test is wholehearted giving.

options.text: God forbids ever trusting Him for provision.
options.note: The verse does the opposite, inviting them to trust His provision.

quiz.q: How should this passage be applied today?

options.text: As a call to trust God's provision and give generously, not as a formula for guaranteed wealth.
options.note: The New Testament reframes giving as cheerful and generous, trusting the faithful Provider.

options.text: As a promise that every gift returns a financial profit.
options.note: That prosperity reading misuses the text; the heart is trust, not gain.

options.text: As a rule that Christians must tithe exactly ten percent or be cursed.
options.note: The tithe law was given to Israel; the New Testament calls for proportionate, cheerful giving.

options.text: As irrelevant, since God no longer cares about our giving.
options.note: God still calls His people to trust Him with their resources.

quiz.q: How is the unchanging God of verse 6 revealed in Christ?

options.text: Jesus Christ is the same yesterday, today, and forever, the steadfast mercy that spares sinners.
options.note: Hebrews 13:8 identifies Christ with the unchanging God of this verse.

options.text: Christ shows that God changes His mind constantly.
options.note: The opposite: Christ reveals God's unchanging faithfulness.

options.text: Christ makes God's promises uncertain.
options.note: In Christ every promise is Yes; His constancy secures them.

options.text: Christ has no link to God's changelessness.
options.note: He is the very expression of the unchanging God.

questions: God says because I do not change, you have not been destroyed. How does God's unchanging faithfulness give you security when your own faithfulness wavers?
God calls, return to Me and I will return to you. Is there an area where you have drifted and need to turn back, trusting that God moves toward those who move toward Him?
The people withheld from God what was His. What does your giving reveal about whether you trust God as your provider or lean on your possessions?
God invited His people to test His faithfulness through obedience. Where is God calling you to step out in trust and prove His provision rather than playing it safe?
We cannot out-give the God who gave His own Son. How does the generosity of God in Christ free you to hold your resources with open hands?

translationNotes.note: I the LORD do not change affirms God's constancy of character and covenant, the ground of His mercy toward the descendants of Jacob.
Blessing without measure carries a BSB footnote noting the literal sense, blessing for which there will be no room, a picture of overflowing abundance.
The devourer carries a BSB footnote noting it is probably a name for a crop-destroying pest, such as locusts, that God promises to rebuke.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Malachi 3:6-12. All 7 verses reproduced in order. No em dashes occur in this range. BSB footnotes at 3:10 (literal blessing for which there will be no room) and 3:11 (the devourer as a crop-destroying pest) are reflected in the translation notes.
